has basen full of discouragement for the
men who are carrying stocks. They
have been forced to witness the gradual
melting away of values for ‘many
montas, until, in the opinion of many,
the bottom was close at hand. The

were sated with profits, and apparently
inclined to the belief that it was about
time to put on the brakes and slow
down, as there seemed 10 be a cessation
of offerings, and, In some instances,
they had been trimmed good and proper
in their short selling.

The long-continued slump has been an
awful strain on the insiders, as the
Street terms the big men who control
the various groups of railroads. New
low records have been made in nearly
everything during the past week, and
nothing has been safe from the raids
of the bear element. Their Canadian
Pacific experience did mot make them
quit attacking high-grade rails, and they
have been operating in the Pacifics, in
Pennsylvania, New York Central, Read
ing, and other leaders. The low-priced
shares have found little favor in this
market, and have heen permitted to sag
and sag again, going in thelr own sweet
way toward a lower level.

CHICAGO, Oct, 12.—Stuyvesant Fish
came to Chicago tonight to open his
ficht on Bdward H. Harriman and Pres.
ident James T. Harahan for the control
of the Illinois Centra! »iilway Com
pany. At the same tim¢ ame the an
nouncement that Mr. J riman would
reacn Chicago Monday, 4 that A. G.
Hackstaff, Eastern fleld irsnal for the
‘Harriman side of the coil oversy, would
reach the city by tomorrow.
The first actual hostilities on the
ground opened when Mr. Fisa reached
the Annex at 7 o'clock. After a hurried
dinner. he had a number of advisers
with him, and the actual task of pre
paring for ednesday's battle, when
the stockholders of the road meet to
elect four directors, was under way long
before midnight.
Would Save Own Scalp.
At first it seemed that tne former
president would take to the warpath
after his enemies. As the time drew
on, however, the war paint faded. It
became more evident that he was in a
struggle to save hile own scalp. There
came strong evidences from the Harri
man-Harahan camp that Fisa would
have his hands full to retain a place
on the board of directors. Mr. Fish did
not himeelf have any particular rebut
ting evidence to offer to the apparent
advantage of his hated rivals,
President Harahan, at his office,
smoked and looked pleased with the
situation. He sald that he was. He
left the irapression that his side of the
fight would have a majoritv of the
950,400 votes Wednesday in the big fight.
He showed more contentment with the
situation than did Mr. Fish.
Has Enough Strength.
“How many proxies have your peo
ple?” he was asked. o
“Enough,” he said.
“Will the use of tne Union Pacific's
stock be enjoined by Mr. Fish?”
There came some talk that a deal
might be made wllereby Mr. Fish might
retain his place on the board. It brought
no contirmatory statements from any
source. The four directors who retire
are: Stuyvesant Fish, A. G. Hackstaff,
H. Harriman, and John Jacob Astor.
r. Hackstaff is filling the unexpired
term of the late John C. Welling.

“Who is Bom~> Isler, who came with

The test of the work of a Legislature,
whether it is good or bad, is how if
wears on the people. Does anybody sup
pose that any of the laws enacted b
the last Legislature increasing tne ef
ficiency of the several schools of the
State, including the public schools, will
be repealed by any future Legisiature?
Jas tne Legislature passed any laws
naterially affecting the welfare of peo
ole of the State that any future Legisla
ture dare repeal, unless they are re
pealed to make them more up to date
and to conform with present-day condi
tions, just as that much-zbused back
tax law of Governor Johnston was re
pealed to make way for another more
stringent, and more effective, looking
to up-to-date methods of achieving the
same ends the back-tax law was enacted
to accomplisa?

n to sum up the the present Leg
ture has accomplished, and compare

ance of good and ive legislation on

NEW YORK MONEY

NEW YORK, Oct, 12.—Money on call
was quoted nominally at 5 per cent to
day. Rates during the week have rangéd
between and 2% per cent, averaging 5.
The whole market was active through
out the week, with a hardening tend
ency. Rates were yery firm. On Mon
day loans were made ar and 4%; Xyges
day, at 6 and 5 per cent; Wednesday, 6
and 38; Thupsday, 6 and 4 and Friday,
at 4% wud 4%.
Time money was firm thpaughout the
week. Demand was not pressing, but
offerings were meager, and borrowers
continued to show a preference for loans
of January maturity. Rates on ninety
day loans were advanced to 6% per
cent. Offerings at 7 per cent for this
class of loans did not meet with muca
response. Rates for four to six month
maturities were generally 6 per cent.
Mercantile paper continued duil and
unchanged throughout the week. A lit
tle buying was reported from the
smaller country banks, but the volume
of business was small. Rates 7 per
cent for four to six months’ bille receiv
able, and prime single names; 7% per
cent for others.
Sterling was firm, with demand bills
10 points higher at 4.86%.

It was not to be expected taat matters
in the local securities market during the
past week would be specially encourag

to expect any bullish sentiment. Condi
tions in Wall Strect were so unsatisfac

optimistic person *o believe that Wash
ington could e¢scupe from the storm
without feeling at least the edge of it.
That prices held so well was due large
ly to the fact that for some weeks past
there has been but little specuiative

At 4 o'clock yesterday afternoon
Louis Rosenthal, convicted by a, Jury
on Friday afternoon of having com
mitted Indecent exposure, was sen
tenced to serve six months In .the
county jail at Tombstone and pay a
fine of $180. He was committed to
the""brancn county jail, pending trans
fer to Tombstone this morning. It Is.
probable that the attorneys .for the
defense will make, an attempt to ob
tain a release on a writ, of habeas
corpus", plan9 for such action having
been made yesterday.
On Friday night after the verdict w,
the jury was rendered the attorneys
for the defense gave notice that on
yesterday at 11 o'clock they would
file motion to set aside the verdlct-l
on the ground that one of the jury-
l men. after being sworn in, had been
allowed to separate from the others.
Yesterday morning Assistant District
Attorneys Ross and Taylor were on
hand to resist any such motion, but
the defense did not file it.
During the afternoon a rumor be-,
came current that Rosenthal Intend
ed to forfeit his ball bond of $500,
and get out ot the city before the
time of passing sentence arrived, but!
at 4 o'clook tba prisoner arrived in
court, and it was found that the ru
mor had been circulated by A. J.
Hepner, his room mate, who was
also arraigned in court .yesterday
morning on a charge of indecent ex
posure. Because of the helnousness of the
offense, and the disgusting detalb the
court gave the prisoner the limit in
jail sentence, and in addition imposed
the. fine. v
It was learned last evening that it
is very probable an effort will be
made" to have' the prisoner Indicted
of a felony charge at the next ses
sion of the grand jury, as several
other Instances where Jie has annoyed
la'die3 of the cfty and subjected them
to Indignities have been brought to
light.
When asked about the matter be
fore leaving for his home. "In Douglas
last evening Assistant District At
torney Ross said:
"At the present time It is Impossi
ble to tell, what action will be taken
against Rosenthal. It is very prob
able that he will have,to serve out the
jail sentence wb.lch.Sas been Imposed,
which will give the Jistrict attorney's
office time to investigate the -various
other cases in which he is alleged to
have committed the same Offense, and
I can assure you that the most severe
action warranted by the premises will
be taken."

Although the local mining compan
ies several months ago discontinued
the practice of paying their employes
In drafts drawn on their New York

which, was made necessary by the
financial stringency, there are still a
number Kot the drafts outstanding,
and there seenw to b.e no disposition
on the part of the holders to sur
render them.
At ths time of the financial panic
In New York when the banking insti
tutions ot that city were forced to
adopt the clearing house rule be
cause of the wild stampede of peo
ple, who had lost faith in almost ev
erthing and were desirous of having
their money in coin, .and the" conse
quent adoption of the rule through
out the country, the local mining
companies for the convenience of
their employes adopted the draft sys
tem. According to this employes, were
paid the same as usual, but instead
of receiving cash received denomina
tional drafts drawn on New York
banks which were received In the
course of business in the district at
their full face value.
These drafts circulated the samo
as money, and in due time the major
ity of theln found their way Into New
York,- and were retired from circu
lation. However there are still out
standing a number of these drafts
and the officials of the company are
desirous of having them presented so
that the owners can receiye their full
face value In cash, and the emer
gency checks can be retired. No doubt
some of these drafts have been lost
or destroyed and will never be cash

EARLY UNIONISM.
Rev. Charles Stetzle Talks of its
First Policies.
On account of the viciousness
of the judges and the manifestly
unfair treatment of the employers.
Parliament in repealed all
the Combination Laws, giving la
bor the utmost liberty in organiz
ing their unions. Organizations
of workingmen sprang up in all
parts of the country. The new
libertv was abused, and inexper
ienced leaders became arrogant
and unreasonable. This gave the
employers an opportunity to de
mand "a return to the old regime,
but in spite of political trickery
and manipulation of every con
ceivable kind, they were unsuc
cessful. Then followed a brief
period of business depression
which gave the employers an op
portunity to deal with their em
ployees in an independent man
ner, with the result that the work
ers again became disheartened,
because of the feeling that the
new law had not proven to be as
beneficial as they had supposed
it would be.
At about this time there was
introduced among the organized
workingmen of England a semi
political organization called the
"National Association for the
Protection of Labor." Establish
ed in 1SS0, it had in a very short
time a membership of one hun
dred thousand, but it was soon
disbanded. Put three years later
there was formed the "Grand Na
tional Consolidated Trades Un
ion." Within a few weeks half
a million workers became identi
fied with it. Skilled and un
skilled workers alike were receiv
ed, and many women. The or
ganization was crudly socialistic
in character, the hope being that
"the workers would gain control of
all the means of production, not
through the use of the ballot, for
they were practically all disfran
chised, but through a monster
strike. This organization also
failed, but the political spirit
aroused during the discussions
consequent upon its establish
ment gave rise to an agitation
favorable to the srantinsr of the
franchise to the working-people of
Great Britain. JUany ot the men
threw themselves into the Chart
ist Movement, and from lS37"to
1842 there developed among them
a revolutionary spirit which
threatened to result in exeat
J
bloodshed. The Chartists, while
not a part of the trades union
movement, nevertheless captured
manv of its leaders and influenced
many workingmen to take radi
cal steps toward securing a com
plete change in the form of gov
ernment. They sought to engage
the workers in general strikes
'until the Charter becomes the
law of the land." In many cases
this movement was repudiated bv
Jocal labor unions, in some in
stances because of the general
rule that religion and politics wert
debarred from the meetings ot
organized labor, but principally
because the organized working-
men as a whole did not have very
much confidence in the move
ment. Begun with a high motive
and with genuine democracy as
its basis, the Chartist Movement
soon degenerated into a species
of economic quackery.
Not a Kick.
Out of the first batch of ap
pointments announced by Governor-elect
Aldrich of Nebraska,
Louis V. Guye of Omaha, mem
ber of the barbers union and
former president of the C. L. U.,
gets the position of deputy labor
commissioner in place of W. M.
Maupin of Lincoln. As yet we
have not heard Bill complain of
the "system." Easton, Pa., La
bor Journal.
Not a complaint And you'll
not hear one from "Bill." He re
tires from office without a single
oang of regret, and without hav
ing made the slightest effort to
retain his position. While hold
ing down the job he tried to per
form his duties as best he could,
and he isn't a bit ashamed of the
record he made. He is succeeded
by a good union man, for which
fact he is thankful he returns to
orivate life and private business
with the knowledge that the chiet
charm about public office holding
is the anticipation the reality is
a bit different. By the way,
Shorpe, did you ever head "Bill"
complain about anything?
Death of Mrs. Johns.
Mrs. C. C. Johns of Grand Is
land died at St. Elizabeth's hos
pital Thursday morning after a
lingering illness. Mrs. Johns was
brought to Lincoln the day after
Christmas, suffering from cancer
of the bowels. An operation was
imperative, but owing to her de
creased vitality, due to Tong ill
ness, she was unable to rally after
the operation. The remains were
taken to Havelock, Ia.,N Thursday
afternoon to be buried in the fam
ily lot. Mrs. Johns was the wife
of C. C. Johns, secretary-treasurer
of the Nebraska Press association
and advertising manager for Wol
bach Bros of Grand Island. She
was an especial favorite in social
circles and among the members of
the press association. To the be
reaved husband and relatives the
sincere sympathy of the newspa
per profession of Nebraska is extended.

Local advocates of the land

Last Wednesday the United
States senate passed the Burkett
bill requiring interstate railroads
to equip their locomotives with
safe and suitable boilers, and ap

service rules. The bill passed
without a negative vote and now
goes to the house. Senator Bur

quest of 60,000 locomotive engin
eers, and has consistently urged
the measure at every opportunity.

BIG CHICAGO BANKER SAYS THE NEW MONEY
LAW WILL HELP BUSINESS QUICKLY
Edmund D. Hulbert, one of Chi
cago's big bankers, and vice presi
dent of the Merchants' Loan Trust
Co., takes an optimistic view of the
business situation and the effect of
the new currency bill on the country.
Mr. Hulbert is one of the big bank
ers of the country, who, instead of
knocking, has done all he could to
help President Wilson fix up a cur
rency bill that would help the coun
try. Banker Hulbert made the follow
ing statement to The Day Book to
day: "The new currency measure is
bound to be a success, because it was
drawn fairly and in the open," he
said. "It's passage should bring in a
Christmas spirit of optimism to re
place a feeling of depression that was
based on nothing at all."
"It will take at least three months
to put the new system at work, but
business will not wait the complete
organization of the new federal re
serve board and of the regional re
serve banks. In my opinion there
will be a response within the near
future that will mean increased busi
ness activity.
"Much of the success of the new
system will depend upon the charac
ter of the men on the reserve board.
But the country knows that the
president will appoint only high-class
mer
"The ultimate effect of the new
system will be good. The Owen-Glass
bill, even its most bitter enemies ad
panics. Banks will not have to be
quite so conservative; there will be
a moderate expansion of credit, and
within a short time business houses
whose stocks are now nearly deplet
ed, will become active" buyers."
Hulbert charged that much of the
opposition to the new currency law
that was partially responsible for
business depression was insincere
and fostered by New York financial
powers that felt that their control of
the money market .was in danger.
"The objection that if the bankers
were not allowed to name the re
serve board;,, it would bo dominated
by a clique df politicians was pure
political buncombe for campaign pur
poses," said he. "Under the present
system, with a single man, the comp
troller of currency, directing finan
cial affairs, how much simpler it
would-be for politicians or the presi
dent to play politics."
Hulbert, it Is reported in Chicago
financial circles, is on a tentative
slate of members of the new federal
reserve board, prepared by President
Wilson.

AGRICULTURE TAUGHT
IN ALL STATES, SHOWS
RESULTS, SAYS NOLAN
(By Associated Press)
URBANA. 111., Dec. 10. The newest
set of agricultural teachers those who
have come to the farm boys at their
work in the open country are making
a great success, according to A. W.
Nolan, assistant professor of agricul
tural extension at the University of
Illinois.
"Since the passage of the Smith
Hughes vocational education act in
1917, teachers of agriculture are now
at work in all the states of the Union".
Professor Nolan said. "These teach
ers are employed for 12 months in the
year. They are paid partly from local
funds and partly from federal funds
granted by the vocational act.
"Under the provisions of this law,
all students enrolling in vocational
agriculture, with teachers thus em-
pioyea, must carry on ior six uiuhuis
of the year, as required part of their
course, a farm project under the super
vision of the teacher.
Teacher Lives in Work.
"A teacher of agriculture literally
lives with his boys during the summer
months, directing their work, study
mid business in connection with their
farm projects. The boys in these
classes are usually organized into vo
cational clubs under which auspices
trips are taken to sales, fairs, uni
versities, agricultural exhibits, and
pood farms. All of this reacts to the
moral and social, as well as the voca
tional and agricultural education of
the boys.
"For the fiscal year ending June SO,
1920. nearly 2,000 schools had estab
lished departments of vocational
agriculture and over 45,000 boys had
enrolled in the work, carrying on the
farm practice as required. The Feder
al Board reported for the year that
the income from the projects of the
boys carrying on the farm practice
was approximately $1,000,000, a cum of
money larger than the federal grant
to the States for carrying on the
work in agriculture."

Cattle Men Want Cheaper Meat.
We don't know just how it all came
about, not being present at the meet

the cattle men from twelve western
states, v ho concluded their convention

manded cheaper meat.

They asked that Attorney General

this instance was that the cattle rais-;

are going into bankruptcy, at least
many of them are, or have been driven
out of the business in order to make
a living.

eral taxes, E. E. Rhodes, vice-president
of the Mutual Benefit Life Insurance
company, Newark, N. J., at its four
teenth annual convention, told the
Association of Life Insurance Presi
dents here today.
Although emphasizing the multiple
taxation of life Insurance, Mr. Rhodes
urged that it is far better that insur
ance men shall sincerely and whole
heartedly co-operate with the govern
ment in finding a reasonable basis of
taxation than that they should take
an antagonistic position toward all
plans that may be presented.
"What is the position of one who
has sought insurance protection, in so
far as federal taxes are concerned?"
asked the speaker. "In the first place,
he pays a tax at the rate of eighty
cents per $1,000 of insurance for the
privilege of insuring his life. In the
second place, he pays a personal tax
upon so much of his income as is
required to pay the premium upon bis
insurance. In the third place, the
amount he pays as a premium is again
i taxed when it reaches the company of
which he is a member. In the fourth
place, if the assessment of an Excess
profits tax is upheld by the courts the
premium is again taxed. In the fifth
place, so much of his premium as is
held by the company for contingencies
is again taxed as a capital stock tax.
In the sixth place, when the amount
inshred is paid to his estate it is
again taxed.
"This is taxation with a vengeance.
The insured is caught coming, stand
ing still and going, in a transaction
which he does not enter into for profit
nd in which there can be no profit."
Devoting his attention further to a
consideration of Federal Income Tax
Law, Mr. Rhodes not only called into
question its constitutionality, so far as
it affects life insurance, but gave an
anaylsis of the inequality which he
said results to the various companies
from the actual operation of the law.
FEDERAL DEPARTMENT OF
FORESTRY RECOMMENDED
HARRISBURG. Pa.. Dec. 10. Fores
ers of the various states in conference
here today were asked by John H.
Wallace, Alabama, conservation com
missioner and said to be the only
elective conservation official in the
country, to urge immediate action for
the creation of a federal department
of conservation to include natural resources.
SUGAR IS COMING.
(By Associated Press)
MEXICO CITY, Dec. 10. Encourag
ing reports have been received from
the states of Sonora and Sinloa, where
the return of prosperity is noted by
the announcement that these estates
expect to export 15,000 carloads of
agricultural products to the United
States within the next few months.
It is said that the state of Sinloa
will have at least 2,000 tons or refined
sugar available for export. The rail
roads of the region are making great
effort to provide the necessary trans
portation fecilities.

Clint and Bessie Robbins will pre
sent the newest play they have ob
tained at the Opera House Thursday,
January 6th. This play is new in
every way, as it combines broad farce,
melo-drama, high class comedy and
romantic drama in its construction.
David Belasco was the sponsor for
the play and it has been a -big suc
cess for Clint and -Bessie Robbins
and their company. Bessie Robbins
is seen as "The Girl,” a child whose
father and mother were killed in an
attack by Villa and his band in Mex
ico. The father being an army officer
of the U. S. A., she is taken by an
old Mexican renegade and brought up
in ignorance of who she is. Circum
stances offer an opportunity to place
her in better surroundings, by a
young lawyer and newspaper man, and
he takes the chance because he feels
the child should be rescued frem such
environment. There are many twists
and turns in the unfolding of the story
and at the climax of the last act comes
the "big” moment of the play. Bessie
Robbins wears an old fashioned gown
In this scene that develops the plot
with lightning rapidity.

KNOW YOUR COW
If it were possible to limit the num
ber of requisites of success hi dairy
dom to one, that one would bo “Know
your cows.” says Thomas M. Olson,
instructor in dairy husbandry at State
college. Heavy feeding, or feeding a
balanced ration does not assure sue
cess, although it will increase some
what the production of the dairy herd.
Pure bred dairy cows do not guarantee
success, not even pure bred cows from
high producing ancestry. Expensive
dairy barns and equipment will prob
ably make dairying more ini'Testing,
and increase somewhat the total pro
duction, but it is not a requisite foi
success. Many great dairy cows have
been developed and have produced
wonderful records in barns that would
not measure up to the standard so far
as up-to-date dairy barns go.
No dairy cow has ever produced her
maximum, unless her feeder knew her.
Knowing her means more than simply
calling her by name and reciting the
names of her ancestors. It means
understanding her every need, des,re,
and condition. For after all, the dairy
cow is an individual, and as such pos
sesses individuality. It seems well
nigh unbelievable that dairymen will
continue groping in the dark on this
all important matter, when by means
of a pair of scales, a Babcock testing
outfit and a little work, every dairy
man could know what each cow in his
herd produced, and thus have a sound
basis on which to feed and judge the
individutil merits of each cow in his
herd.

ished. The revolutionary possibilities

ether features which would readily

BY G. GOULD LINCOLN.
Republican control of Congress Is
threatened in Tuesday's election, with
depression and prohibition the chief
contributing factors.
Democrats are due to make heavy
Inroads into big Republican majorities
In both House and Senate.
While these Democratic galas are to
be large, there appears to be only a
passibility, not a probability, that a
clear majority will be obtained by the
Democrats in either House of Congress.
Owing to discontment because of eco- J
nomic conditions in the country, how
ever. the swing to the Democrats may
yet assume landslide proportions. If it
does, possibility will become an accom
plished fact. The Republican majority
in the House will be wiped out. A
Democratic majority in the Senate is
more remote possibility than a Demo
cratic majority in the House.
4 Controlled Since 1918.
Control of both houses of Congress
has rested with the Republicans since

Absentee Votes Are Dispatched to
California by Airmail.
Three days ahead of time, President
and Mrs. Hoover marked their ballots
yesterday for the general elections.
Voting on absentee ballots forwarded
from Palo Alto, Calif., the White House
occupants made their choice and then
Immediately dispatched their votes by
airmail back to California. They are
expected to reach the proper polls by
Tuesday easily.
The privacy of the ballot was respect
ed even for the President, and no one
asked him if he had expressed his
preference for the straight Republican
ticket.

A safeguard for sleep walkers, ty

almost invisible light beams instead
of ropes or strips of bedcloths, has
been devised by a British manu
facturer of photo-electric cells and
similar devices, states a London
correspondent of the Chicago Trib
une.
One or more light beams of dim
blue or red light are directed across
the bed from special lamps and re
flectors like miniature searchlights.
These beams enter one or more
light-sensitive cells, which give an
electric current so long as the light
beam enters them. If anything in
terrupts the light, beam, even for
a small fraction of a second, the
electric signal ceases. This stoppage
may be made to sound an alarm or
to work any other kind of electric
apparatus.
When the sleeper retires this light
beam system is switched on. If then
the sleeper gets out of bed or even
sits up in bed, his body must cut one'
or more of the light beams passing
across the bed. This casts a shadow

National Topics Interpreted

National Press Building Washington, D. C.
Washington.—Supporters of the
New Deal, as well as its opponents,
v t are watching a
New new experiment
Experiment by President
Roosevelt with
more than passing interest. They
are watching this experiment be
cause of various circumstances, in
cluding obviously its political
phases, for they realize that the
President has stepped out into a
hitherto untrod field, unplowed
ground, in this new experiment.
Chiefly because it is something
entirely new and not because any
one yet can tell what is going to
happen, I want to report this week
on the President’s plan to have his
eldest son, James, his No. 1 sec
retary, serve as a clearing house
between the Chief Executive and
some eighteen or more government
agencies. Roosevelt, the younger,
has been designated by the Presi
dent to sit down once each week
with heads of each of the enumerat
ed agencies and talk over their
problems, iron out those which he
can help solve, co-ordinate the work
between them as far as he is able
to do so, and, most important to the
men with whom he deals, decide
which questions confronting these
units of government may be matters
for the President’s personal consid
eration.
It is a big order. But Jimmy,
as he is known around here, is a
big man, at least physically, be
cause he stands something like six
feet, one inch. He is twenty-nine
years old.
The audiences at the White House
with Jimmy have already started
and the agency heads who have
sat through the conferences seem
well pleased. At least, there
has been no criticism yet, no signs
of jealousy or indications of dis
sent among those who are called
upon to take up their problems
with the young secretary to the
President. In fact, I have heard in
only a few places a revival of the
punning that went out a couple of
years ago when the “Roosevelt for
King” game was in its heyday, and
Jimmy was laughingly labeled as
the crown prince. Thus, the plan
surely seems to have started off
most auspiciously. But as I said,
what success, what trials and tribu
lations, await it, no one can say.

Undoubtedly, President Roosevelt
has started a plan of action that has
_. much to be said in
Much in its favor. It has,
lt Favor of course, many
possibilities of
trouble, and there surely is argu
ment against having the White
House secretariat determine policy
when the President has been elected
to that job. Yet, at this time,
credit must be argued along with
signs and portents of trouble.
First, let us recognize that the of
fice of President of the United States
is a man-killing job. There is noth
ing like it anywhere else in the
world. There are not too many men
who can stand up under the load
of work and worry that is contin
ually on the shoulders of the head
of the nation. Thera is no way
possible for him to escape it. Vaca
tions? Sleep? Absence from Wash
ington? Not a chance. The work
and worry is with him always, ev
ery minute out of his twenty-four
hour day.
Thus, if there is any way that
can.be devised to lift some of the
burden, it ought to be done. No>
way has been found heretofore,
however, and the millstone contin
ues around the President’s neck.
On the other hand, this is a de
mocracy. In the opinion of many
persons, there already is too much
power lodged in the hands of in
dividuals who were not elected, but
were appointed, to office. The
President has vast power and he
has to unload it somewhere into the
hands of those he appoints. Never
theless, throughout the federal gov
ernment scores of men and wom
en exercise very great authority
over your life and mine who were
personally selected by the Presi
dent—for whom none of us voted
when we voted for a presidential
nominee. Result: red tape, rules
and regulations, do’s and don’ts ga
lore, until we hardly knew whether
we can eat our food without trans
gressing or failing to comply with
some federal regulation.
All of that is byway of saying
that by implementing the relation
ship between the President and men
and women whom he has appoint
ed to office through the insertion of
an added wheel of authority, there
lie potential changes in all of
these governmental policies. And
there lies the possibility of changes
being made, for better or tor worse,
without the President having knowl
edge of them.
What of the personal side—the
feelings of the officials who have
to deal with a young man who can
speak, to some extent at least, for
his father, the President? As I said
earlier, thus far none of the con
ferees have given any indication ol
disturbed feelings. They may never
have any thoughts along this line.
Yet, I venture this observation: if
they don’t resent having the Presi
dent’s son tell them what to do, or
not to do, at some time or other,
the officials concerned will be unlike
most other human beings.

THE PREVENTION OF WAR
OUR NATION watched with greatest concern
the recent sessions of the Council of Foreign
ministers in London and welcomed the report of
our Secretary of State. Even though the confer
ence ended without agreement on important am
urgent issues, we were relieved to know thai
there had been no compromise of principles.
Unless international relations and institutions
are founded on justice and respect for the rights
of all nations, we shall be unable to prevent wars
,and to carry out the provisions of the Atlantic
Charter for which the United Nations pledged
themselves in the recent war. Where appeasement
leads we know’ from our tragic experience of the
Thirties, when democratic governments did not
insist upon principles in dealing with Hitler, per
mitting Nazi control to be imposed on neighbor
ing countries.
The first meeting of the Council was ob
viously exploratory and a testing out of motives.!
From the next meeting we may expect agree
ments. It is obvious that the democratic forces
of the world need to mobilize their influence to
reinforce our representatives who make decisions
on our behalf. To enable us to function, our rep
resentatives should insist that the Council of For
eign Ministers issue daily communiques and make
public immediately all proposals and agreements.
Citizens of many nations have made sacri
fices and given their lives for world peace and
justice. An obligation rests on us to see to it that
«our great purpose is not frustrated by secret
agreements or by compromising principles.
We can prevent war through the sincere co
operation of all nations. We shall lose the peace
af we permit nations to develop spheres of influ
ence through control over other nations. The
^United States has great economic and financial
■resources which can be used to support democratic
principles. We must first help liberated peoples to
return to a self-supporting basis and give them
opportunities for a democratic way of life, so that
the whole world may move forward to new levels
of prosperity. We need to do away with the bar
riers that separate nations—whether barriers to
trade or to free access to information. There must
be opportunity for free reporting of the news in
every country.
The spirit of the United Nations we hope to
create will be born of cooperation and nurtured
by understanding and goodwill.
It cannot be nurtured by suspicion, intrigue
and double-dealing, the instruments of power pol
itics employed to gain power over the lives of
others.
Our purpose is to make all free, with full re
sponsibility for living in peace with all other na
tions of goodwill. V

NEEDED UNION SERVICE
DECAUSE of Congressional reluctance to pro
0 vide adequate unemployment compensation
for the reconversion period, unemployed workers
must now look to the state laws. These laws are
inadequate in coverage, in tricky exemption which
prevents all in the labor market from qualifying
in duration and in amount. They usually compen
sate for only a small fraction of the wage loss.
In view of the stand taken by Congress,
unions should plan to utilize to the fullest the in
adequate insurance available. One of the basic
difficulties to be overcome is the attitude of many
administrators who mistakenly regard the unem
ploymept funds as the property of employers.
Tliis attitude results from state laws that re
duce rates for employers whose funds are not used
to pay benefits. Such provisions defeat the basic
purpose of unemployment insurance, which is to
provide income for self-supporting workers until
they can again find suitable jobs.
In the majority of the states unemployment
compensation reserves accumulate from the three
per cent of payrolls paid in by employers. In a
few states employes contribute jointly with em
ployers. In any case, the funds are deferred wages,
laid aside for the sole purpose of providing in
comes for workers during periods of unemploy
ment. Workers help to earn these deferred wages
just as they earn the wages that go into the pay
envelopes.
If unions are to get this philosophy of un
employment compensation accepted by its admin
istrators, they must select some representative
to advocate the cause of those members registered
for insurance benefits.
In the past many workers denied benefits by
the administrator did not know they could appeal
their cases or how to appeal. If the union presses
for appeal in cases unfairly determined, it will
force better administration.
A key decision in administration is the work
er’s right to refuse a job which is not suitable.
As a condition of approval by the Social Security
Board, state laws must incorporate federal stan
dards on this point. The worker may reject jobs
on which industrial disputes exist or if the wages,
hours or other conditions of work are substan
tially less favorable than those prevailing for
similar work in the locality or if employment is
conditioned on joining a company union or refus
ing to join a union. Workers may refuse jobs
which do not require their highest skill without
disqualification for benefits for a period of time
varying from state to state, as determined by ad
ministration. There is always a chance to argue
to the longest duration. On the federal standards
issue appeals can be taken to the Social Security
Board.
Unions that provide alert, aggressive and effi
cient service for their members in this important
field will help to improve administration as well
as accumulate information useful for amending
the law.
AFL HELPS VETERANS
VARLY in 1945, the American Federation of La
bor launched a country-wide program of assis
tance to returning veterans. The Labor League
for Human Rights joined in this emportant en
deavor.
This program began with the formation of
veterans’ committees within AFL central bodies.
Primary duties were to help veterans find satis-
than life.
Today, many of these committees maintain
offices within labor headquarters others serve
in advisory capacities in centers sponsored by
Community Chests or municipal governments.
These AFL Labor League Committees have now
grown to maturity. Multiple contracts with re
turnees has given them background for substan
tial assistance. They are serving with great bene
fit to the veteran and with outstanding credit to
themselves.
The Labor League for Human Rights has
completed a survey of observations by these com
mittees embracing 57 cities in 23 states. The sur
vey shows that the major need of millions now
receiving discharges is essentially the same as
the thin stream of returnees several months ago.
Happily, the survey specifically reveals that
the committees have been successful in placing
thousands of veterans not previously members of
unions on jobs they desired. This favorable situa
tion has resulted because of the liberal policies of
most AFL unions in extending full membership
rights to veterans without cost. To the uninform
ed this might appear a trival gesture, but in most
cases the gratuitious membership is a generous
presentation of union services.
Half of the 12,000,000 members of the armed
forces have been demobilized. This leaves huge
numbers yet to return. To help them select a suit
able trade or occupation will be a challenge each
community cannot fail to meet, and will require
the cooperation of all interested agencies.
ASSOCIATION TOLERATES CORRUPTION
ME ARE sure readers will acquit us of any de
sire to embarass the distinguished gentlemen
who compose the American Bar Association. How
ever, we feel it our duty to call attention to the
fact that Harry S. Knight, secretary of the Ameri
can Bar Association, has just been convicted in
Federal court in Scranton, Pa., of the rather ser
ious charge of “fixing” Federal Judge Albert W.
Johnson in order that Mr. Knight’s client might
get the decision he desired. w
Of course, the conviction of this distinguish
ed member of the American Bar Association does
not prove that all lawyers are crooks. It does
prove, however, that the American Bar Associa
tion, fond of lecturing others, makes little or no
effort to clean its own house.
The Bar Association took no notice of the
appalling conditions prevailing in Judge Johnson’s
court. When Harry Sauthoff, then Congressman
from Wisconsin, made a partial exposure on the
floor of the House, he didn’t receive the least bit
of aid or encouragement from the Bar Association.
Instead, the Bar Association retained Mr.
Knight as its secretary after he had been indicted
by a Federal grand jury and while it was a matter
of common knowledge that he and a number of
other lawyers had been buying court decisions in
Scranton for a considerable period.

FRIDAY, MARCH 7, 1958
ON WITH THE RECESSION
The announcement has been made in
Washington that unemployment in the Uni
ted States has now reached 5,000,000 per
sons. That is a pretty fair percentage of the
total number of working Americans.
This is of great concern to every govern
ment economist and every U. S. citizen be
cause when unemployment reaches this to
tal, with prices and wages continuing to go
up—as sales go down in a number of phases
of the economy—something is obviously out
of kilter.
In fact, the is now at a crucial
point along the road to tomorrow. Either
we have an upturn soon or we move into a
depression. Car inventories are piling up,
steel is slow (estimated at fifty-five per cent
of productive capacity) and plant expansion
is being reduced considerably.
This, combined with a farm recession,
now in its fourth or fifth year, and farm
policies which are failing, necessitate large
governmental outlays to prevent the depres
sion that could occur without heavy federal
spending, including defense spending.
We do not think a depression will follow,
largely because of high levels of defense and
other government spending. But this will
be an artificial prop to the economy, that will
probably cause some more inflation, and
which will probably leave the economy—
eventually—in a more precarious position
than it was in before, or is in right now.
What is needed is permanently sound
farm legislation,* labor legislation, and in
flation controls, so that the eventual read
justment will not be severe. The present ad
justment would be far more severe were it
not for the international situation, armed
services payrolls, unemployment payments,
social security income and local and state
government expenditures, which are at a
high level.
President Eisenhower says the economy
will experience an upturn by the summer,
and hints that he will favor a cut in taxes if
there is no such upturn. However, if there
isn't, Congress will then be adjourning, and
any tax action would have to wait until 1959.
In the meantime, if the Administration is
misjudging the situation, the recession could
grow more severe.
PRODUCTIVE CAPACITY
One of the major questions posed by the
current recession has nothing to do with op
timism or pessimism, faith in America or
lack of faith in America, the election of Dem
ocrats or of Republicans. This question is
factual: Have increases in productive capac
ity outstripped employment?
In other words, can the American econ
omy now produce so much more with fewer
workers than it could 10 years ago that few
er workers are needed in manufacturing in
dustries? Too many people today talk about
the recession and the American economy
without thinking much about automation,
which has been quite rapidly introduced in
America during the last few years. Along
with automation, we are witnessing the de
velopment of new skilled workers, especially
as a result of recent technological advances
and the development of thermonuclear en
ergy. Older skilled and semi-skilled workers
are threatened.
Automation can be a boon if displaced
workers are re-trained for other jobs, and if
the effects of automation are cushioned.
Otherwise, it can wreak havoc on American
working people. These are all factors in
volved in the recession. They should be
thought about seriously, but outside the usu
al framework of politics and.optimism ver
sus pessimism.
A HOPE FOR TAX RELIEF
The steeply graduated rates of our pres
ent income tax structure confiscate up to 91
per cent of individual earnings and levy a
52 per cent tax on corporate income.
Congressman Sadlak, a Republican, and
Congressman Ilerlong, a Democrat, have in
troduced a bill which would remove the road
blocks to the industrial expansion and re
search needed to create more jobs and raise
living standards. Their bill would give tax
relief to both individual and corporate Fed
eral taxpayers, with a 42 per cent top for
both individuals and corporations. The cuts
would be made gradually over a five-year per
iod and because normal economic growth
would expand Federal revenue, the Trea
sury would not suffer.
The Sadlak-Herlong bill would go a long
way toward removing the shadow of confis
catory taxation that looms large over every
business conference table, disrupting indus
try's traditional function of planning and
providing for the expansion, research, plant
maintenance and job opportunities that have
made America the most prosperous nation
on earth.
PROPER TAX CUT BILL
DEPENDS ON HOME SUPPORT
Sentiment in the House Ways and Means
Committee is increasing some form of tax
reduction in the next few months. Members
of that committee were deeply impressed by
the need for tax rate reform during the twen
ty-four days of hearings recently completed.

A large number of representatives of em
ployer groups appeared before the Commit
tee to plead the cause of sound tax rate re
form. They made an excellent impression.
That impression must not be allowed to die
from lack of further support.
If business is to be freed of some of the
straight-jacket restrictions imposed under
the present tax structure, now is the time to
insist upon proper tax legislation. Don't wait
until House members have committed them
selves to the wrong kind of tax reduction
bill.
The need for immediate action is increas
ed by learning of labor leaders' plans. Hun
dreds of union officials are expected to des
cend on Washington the second week in
March. They intend to buttonhole Congress
men and push their plans for HlCOme tax re
duction almost entirely in the low bracket
rates as well as increased unemployment ben
efits, Social Security programs, and other
federal spending programs.
Most favored by business are the Sadlak
Herlong bills (H. R, 6452 and H. R, 9119) to
reduce individual and corporate rates. This
legislation provides for tax reduction over
five years (to be spread to nine years, if nec
essary) of all corporate and individual tax

Not Affected by Recession I During Depression of *3os
By HENRY CATHCART
Central Press Washington Writer
WASHINGTON— The coffee market, vitally important to South
America, is not one of those directly troubled by United
States policies, either foreign or domestic. The Pan-American Coffee
bureau in Washington has come up with an unusual economic in
dicator to show that the present U. S. recession hasn’t sunk to the
depression level yet.
The bureau reports that there is no evidence
yet that coffee sales in the United States have
been seriously affected by the recession, although
prices have been steadily dropping for months
as a result of heavy supplies.
In contrast to present sales, the bureau notes,
during the depression of the 1930 s coffee prices
also dropped to very low levels but consumption
actually increased at that time.
The analysis seems to indicate that people
drink more coffee when they have financial wor
ries in “bad times” than they do in periods of
prosperity.

MILITARY OVERHAUL— Look for the fight
Washington
on reorganization of the Pentagon to. be one of the last items of
business of an unusually long off-year election session of Congress.
The House armed services committee is not expected to complete
Its version of a defense reorganization bill until at least June 1.
Even after House action is completed, the President’s Pentagon
plan faces a formidable hurdle in the Senate, since Armed Services
Chairman Richard B. Russell (D), Georgia, has expressed senti
ments similar to those of his fellow Georgian, Rep. Carl Vinson.
Vinson, the House armed services chairman, is the leader of the
administration’s critics on the military reshuffle issue. All in all,
it will be late July or early August before any compromise is likely
to clear Congress and go to the President.
Congressmen usually like to end election-year sessions by mid-
July so they will have, plenty of time to convince voters they de
•erve to keep their $25,000-a-year jobs.
However, that isn’t likely this year. August 15 is a more frequent
guess for adjournment, partly because of the controversy over the
military reorganization plan.
